themes.title: The rules-based order has always been selectively enforced and structurally unaccountable, but the unprecedented honesty about this failure across both Western and non-Western actors creates genuine space for reform.
themes.abstract: The international order's central structural failure — the absence of enforcement against powerful rule-breakers — is not new but is newly acknowledged by all parties, including the Western states that previously benefited from the fiction of universal enforcement. The West's double standard on Ukraine and Gaza, the Global South's long experience of might-makes-right, and the realization that even the 'hypocritical fiction' of sovereign equality is structurally necessary all converge on a single finding: the order cannot be restored to a golden age that never existed, but the removal of diplomatic pretense may finally create space for genuine reform.

clusters.cluster_title: The rules-based international order has always been selectively enforced, and the current alarm reflects a Eurocentric awakening to a reality the developing world has long experienced.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Multiple speakers converge on the claim that might-makes-right has been the operative principle for weaker states all along, and the perceived 'collapse' of the international order is only front-page news because its dysfunction now affects Europe and the transatlantic world — the rules existed but accountability for powerful violators never did.

clusters.cluster_title: No functioning mechanism exists to hold powerful states accountable for violations of international law, and great powers actively resist creating one.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The central structural failure of the international order is the absence of enforcement against powerful actors who breach the rules — a gap identified as the source of cascading failures — yet every proposal for accountability is blocked by the very states that would be constrained, leaving the question of who enforces the rules against the enforcers unresolved.

clusters.cluster_title: The unprecedented honesty of current international conversations — where all sides openly state how the old system failed them — is genuine cause for optimism despite a dangerous transition.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The removal of diplomatic pretense about the old order's effectiveness creates space for genuine reform, even as the transition period brings heightened danger from economic disparity, technological disruption, and geopolitical realignment.

clusters.cluster_title: The rules-based order needs not restoration but reform that eliminates Western hypocrisies — treating exceptions to Western values as the rules undermines the order's legitimacy and creates vulnerabilities populists exploit.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Both left-wing and right-wing critics converge on the claim that Western hypocrisy — selective application of international law, looking the other way on allied violations — corrodes the rules-based order from within, and the path forward requires not returning to the status quo ante but building a more consistently principled version that can withstand populist and authoritarian challenge.

clusters.cluster_title: The rules-based order was always underwritten by power, and recognizing this does not mean rejecting norms — but abandoning even the fiction of sovereign equality leaves small states no reason to distinguish the US from China.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A tension between the realist claim that rules don't enforce themselves and therefore strong states must underwrite the order, and the counterargument that the 'hypocritical fiction' of equality among nations is structurally essential — because without it, international relations reduces to raw power and small countries lose any reason to prefer American hegemony over Chinese.

clusters.cluster_title: The West's double standard on Ukraine and Gaza is a potential fatal blow to its moral authority with the non-Western world, but Western panels consistently dodge the question.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The question of whether treating Ukraine and Gaza by different principles fatally undermines Western moral authority was repeatedly raised but effectively evaded — with panelists deflecting to irony or pivoting back to Ukraine — leaving unresolved whether addressing the double standard is a prerequisite for credibility with the Global South.

themes.title: Results-driven multilateralism and unconventional diplomacy produce concrete outcomes but systematically exclude the populations whose buy-in determines durability.
themes.abstract: Plurilateral coalitions and Trump's unique leverage have delivered ceasefire agreements and unprecedented Israeli concessions, but these approaches consistently exclude affected parties — most critically Palestinians — whose self-determination and territorial unity remain non-negotiable conditions for lasting settlement, creating a structural gap between diplomatic achievement and sustainable peace.

clusters.cluster_title: The Board of Peace's charter diverges from its UNSC mandate by dropping Palestinian participation, time limits, and Gaza specificity, raising legitimacy concerns about 'focused multilateralism' that excludes affected parties.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The Board of Peace is cited as a flagship of focused multilateralism, but its founding documents intentionally exceed the narrower UNSC Resolution 2803, and the exclusion of Palestinians from the body undermines the legitimacy and buy-in that universal institutions are meant to provide — a tension between results-oriented flexibility and partnership-based legitimacy that was surfaced but not resolved.

clusters.cluster_title: 'Focused multilateralism' through plurilateral groupings is producing results where traditional UN-centered institutions have failed, but its legitimacy depends on equal treatment of states.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A sharp disagreement between the claim that ad hoc plurilateral coalitions are delivering concrete outcomes (ceasefire, critical minerals, regional normalization) and the counterclaim that any reformed multilateral system must preserve state equality and ensure no actor is above the law — with the US framing results as self-justifying and the EU insisting process and legitimacy are inseparable from outcomes.

clusters.cluster_title: Palestinian self-determination and the unity of Gaza and the West Bank are non-negotiable for a lasting settlement, and humanitarian conditions remain dire despite ceasefire.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Even with a ceasefire, aid access remains critically insufficient and people continue to die; stabilizing Gaza is a necessary step toward — not a substitute for — Palestinian self-determination and the territorial unity of Gaza and the West Bank, which Saudi Arabia treats as a non-negotiable condition for normalization.

clusters.cluster_title: Only President Trump could have secured Israeli acceptance of language on no occupation, no annexation, and Palestinian self-determination in a Security Council resolution.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The claim that Trump's unique negotiating position — unmatched by any other international actor — produced unprecedented Israeli concessions on Gaza in a UNSC resolution, including language on Palestinian self-determination, is offered as evidence that unconventional diplomacy delivers results no multilateral process could achieve.

themes.title: The transatlantic burden-sharing debate is not merely about money but about whether alliances are dependencies the US subsidizes or strategic assets that constitute its superpower advantage.
themes.abstract: The factual dispute about what counts as 'paying your share' (defense spending vs. development aid) conceals a deeper disagreement: one side frames allies as free-riders whose passivity necessitated Trump's corrective pressure, while the other argues that voluntary alliances are precisely what differentiates US power from Russian and Chinese coercion — making the treatment of allies as subordinates self-defeating.

clusters.cluster_title: US demands for burden-sharing and UN fiscal reform are framed as justified by massive US contributions and taxpayer interests, but Europe counters that its total multilateral contribution is substantial when development aid is included.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The US argues it has paid more than 180 countries combined into the UN system and that asking 450 million Europeans with a comparable economy to shoulder more defense costs is reasonable given $37 trillion in debt, while the EU responds that counting development aid alongside defense spending shows Europe is already a major contributor — a factual disagreement about what counts as 'paying your share.'

clusters.cluster_title: America's alliances are its unique strategic asset that differentiates it from Russia and China, and treating allies as subordinates undermines US superpower status.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The argument that no country joins Russia or China in war voluntarily but many join the US reframes the dependency narrative: allies are not free-riders but force multipliers, and the US needs partnership-based multilateralism precisely to sustain its advantage over rival powers, especially as China rises.

clusters.cluster_title: The international system has been coming unglued for a decade, and the Trump administration's pressure approach — vindicated by NATO defense spending increases — is a corrective, not a rupture.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The claim that crises predating the current administration (Ukraine, Gaza, Iran, Houthis, Venezuela) prove the old order was already failing, and that sustained US pressure for reform — as with NATO burden-sharing — produces better outcomes than the prior passivity, reframing Trump-era disruption as overdue reform rather than destruction.

themes.title: US reassertion in the Western Hemisphere is welcomed by both Washington and Latin American capitals, but for fundamentally different reasons — the US framing it as overdue leadership and regional partners framing it as continental cooperation.
themes.abstract: Both US and Latin American voices converge on the desirability of renewed hemispheric engagement — citing counter-narcotics, Venezuela, and investment — but the underlying framings differ between American dominance and multilateral partnership, with the 'Americas First' gesture interpreted as either imperial assertion or continental solidarity depending on the speaker.

clusters.cluster_title: Colombia frames US hemispheric engagement as a counter-narcotics partnership opportunity, welcoming 'Americas First' as continental cooperation rather than unilateral dominance.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Colombia reframes US strategic assertiveness in the Western Hemisphere as an opening for partnership on transnational organized crime — the demand-driven drug trade that kills more Americans than any conflict — with the 'Americas First' gesture symbolizing a continental rather than imperial relationship.

clusters.cluster_title: US reassertion in the Western Hemisphere is welcomed both by the US as overdue leadership and by Latin American allies as filling a vacuum — despite different emphases on partnership versus dominance.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Both US and Latin American voices frame renewed US engagement in the hemisphere as positive — the US citing Venezuela, Haiti, and regional summits as proof of leadership, and Argentina citing competitive pressure and investment opportunities — though the underlying framings differ between US dominance and multilateral partnership.

themes.title: Populism is not one phenomenon but at least three — an economic response to elite failure, a political weapon against institutional checks, and a legitimate democratic impulse — and confusing them produces the wrong counter-strategy.
themes.abstract: The clusters collectively reveal that populism has incompatible definitions (legitimate responsiveness vs. institutional destruction), incompatible causes (Democratic betrayal of workers, unmet post-communist expectations, center-left migration failures), and incompatible prescriptions (Milei's deregulation, wealth taxes, EPP firewalls, visionary leadership) — yet the question of whether left- and right-wing populism pose equivalent democratic risks was never resolved, leaving the conceptual confusion itself as the primary obstacle to an effective counter-strategy.

clusters.cluster_title: The Democratic Party's dismissal of populist economic demands like a $15 minimum wage and tuition-free college betrayed its working-class base and opened the door to right-wing populism.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The party that claimed to listen to working people privately derided the economic demands of its grassroots as too extreme, and the resulting perception of betrayal fuels a pendulum of working-class frustration that swings between parties and is exploited by authoritarian movements offering scapegoats rather than solutions.

clusters.cluster_title: Defeating populism requires delivering substantive policy answers to citizens' real concerns — not moralizing — combined with brave leadership that shapes rather than follows public opinion.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The primary antidote to populism is concrete delivery on migration, growth, and bureaucracy (e.g., 80% reduction in Mediterranean crossings, Mercosur deal), paired with visionary leadership in the mold of Adenauer or Kohl that sets the agenda rather than ceding discursive space to populists.

clusters.cluster_title: The EPP's firewall against far-right parties is defined by three criteria — pro-Europe, pro-Ukraine, pro-rule of law — and the center-left's refusal to support strict migration enforcement is feeding the extremism the firewall is meant to contain.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The EPP maintains a firm exclusion of parties like the AfD that fail its pro-Europe, pro-Ukraine, pro-rule-of-law tests (enforced by expelling Orbán), while arguing that the socialists' failure to back mainstream migration enforcement measures like the safe third country concept is itself the driver of populist growth.

clusters.cluster_title: Milei's Argentina is not populism but rules-based capitalism that replaces crony capitalism with free trade and competition, and its political success proves that tangible consumer benefits can sustain reform even under austerity.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Argentina's reforms are distinguished from both economic populism (inflationary short-term spending) and political populism (denial of pluralism) by their emphasis on antitrust, competition, and free trade — and their continued electoral support despite austerity proves that visible consumer benefits like affordable cell phones can substitute for populist spending promises.

clusters.cluster_title: Populism is not inherently negative — responsiveness to people's frustrations is the core of politics — and the real danger arises only when populism is weaponized to undermine democratic institutions.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The distinction between listening to citizens' frustrations (legitimate populism) and using that frustration to attack courts, media, and institutional checks (anti-democratic populism) is essential, and governing responsibility tends to moderate populist leaders — though whether this moderation is sufficient without actively reinforced checks and balances remains unresolved.

clusters.cluster_title: Unmet post-communist expectations — wanting capitalism's benefits with socialism's safety net — created the fertile ground for populism in Central and Eastern Europe.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Citizens in former communist countries expected to gain all the benefits of capitalism while retaining socialist-era security and no personal responsibility, and the resulting frustration when that proved impossible became the raw material populists exploited by scapegoating governments, courts, and media.

clusters.cluster_title: Whether left-wing and right-wing populism pose equivalent democratic risks remains unresolved, as the panel's shared emphasis on working-class grievances obscured the incompatibility of their prescriptions.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A democratic socialist advocating wealth taxes and a Milei supporter advocating deregulation both claim to address the working-class frustrations driving populism, but the session never directly confronted how these competing prescriptions relate to the democratic risks each camp claims to counter — leaving the question of whether 'populism' is a unitary phenomenon unresolved.

themes.title: Extreme inequality, corporate consolidation, and the fiscal exhaustion of the European welfare state are structural economic threats to democracy that neither additional social spending nor austerity alone can address.
themes.abstract: Democratic erosion has material foundations: extreme inequality fuels authoritarian populism, unchecked corporate consolidation produces entities rivaling nation-states, and rising defense budgets threaten to crowd out social spending — yet the European social model has already reached its fiscal ceiling at roughly 40% of GDP, making the American prescription of expanded benefits inapplicable and forcing a search for structural reforms that are politically harder to sell than populist spending promises.

clusters.cluster_title: Extreme income inequality and unchecked corporate consolidation are underdiscussed security threats that drive authoritarianism and undermine democratic governance.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Extreme inequality fuels social instability and authoritarian populism, while unchecked corporate consolidation produces entities rivaling nation-states in power — making antitrust enforcement and a wealth tax matters of democratic security, not just economic policy.

clusters.cluster_title: Rising defense budgets may crowd out social spending unless democracies find ways to make defense investment serve dual economic and social purposes.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A sharp tension between the claim that defense and social spending are inherently in competition (France's debt interest now exceeds its education budget) and the counterclaim that defense industrialization generates tax revenue and EU fiscal rule exemptions ease the trade-off — with the unresolved question of whether democratic resilience requires investing in public services as much as military capability.

clusters.cluster_title: Germany's social model is already maxed out at 40% of GDP, and the center-left cannot win votes by adding social benefits — structural and demographic reform is needed instead.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The European welfare state has reached its fiscal ceiling, making the American prescription of expanded social benefits inapplicable to Europe; what is needed is structural reform that is hard to sell against populist promises but necessary for long-term sustainability.

themes.title: The West's identity is contested not between the US and Europe but within every society simultaneously, and the shift from 'values' to 'civilization' as the organizing frame reflects incompatible political projects with mutually contradictory causal narratives.
themes.abstract: The proliferation of definitions of the West — geopolitical alliance, Judeo-Christian civilization, universal-values community — and competing attributions of blame for its crisis — progressive overreach embedded in institutions, business-class globalization that outsourced jobs, bipartisan failures exploited by a demagogue — reveal that neither side can define the West in a way that survives the other's rebuttal. The administration's rhetorical shift from 'values' to 'civilization' signals a bounded identity project, but its own aggressive nationalism (Greenland, Panama Canal) contradicts the civilizational criteria it claims to champion, and the attempt to redefine American identity from creed to ethnicity is contested as both historically wrong and politically dangerous.

clusters.cluster_title: The West is not splitting between the US and Europe but fracturing internally within every country between competing definitions of Western identity — Cold War, civilizational, values-based — with the division running through each society.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The proliferation of incompatible definitions of 'the West' — geopolitical alliance, Judeo-Christian civilization, universal-values community — means the divide is not transatlantic but intra-societal, running through every country, and self-questioning pluralism has been the West's defining characteristic since the mid-19th century rather than a sign of collapse.

clusters.cluster_title: Post-Cold War progressive commitments were embedded in liberal institutions and transnational governance, eroding national sovereignty, family, borders, and industrial capacity — provoking the current populist-nationalist correction.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A sharp disagreement between the claim that progressive social commitments were 'smuggled' into the post-Cold War order and transnational bodies — weakening the West's fundamental strengths and excluding conservative voices — and the counterclaim that expanding freedom to marginalized groups was a civilizational achievement, not an excess, and that framing it as the problem is revisionist nostalgia for white male privilege.

clusters.cluster_title: The conservative-vs-progressive framing of the West-West divide is misleading because Trump is not a conservative and the actual societal fracture was caused by bipartisan failures — job outsourcing on the right, cultural backlash on the left.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The neat ideological divide between conservatism and progressivism misrepresents the crisis: Trump operates as a demagogue without principles rather than a genuine conservative, and the erosion of the social contract was jointly caused by free-market fundamentalism that outsourced jobs and social progressivism that generated cultural alienation — making the real divide about demagoguery versus a confused opposition.

clusters.cluster_title: Globalization and mass migration were driven by business-class economic interests — cheap labor and profit maximization — not by progressive ideology, undermining the conservative narrative about who caused these disruptions.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The claim that globalization and mass migration were progressive projects is historically false: both were supported by Republican businessmen seeking cheap labor and profit, and characterizing them as left-wing inventions serves a revisionist narrative that deflects responsibility from the economic right.

clusters.cluster_title: Trump's territorial rhetoric on Greenland, the Panama Canal, and the Gulf of America reflects a strategic correction of 1990s overcommitment — but it contradicts the civilizational criteria the administration claims to value.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The administration frames its hemispheric assertiveness as correcting catastrophic post-Cold War neglect of the US neighborhood, but the Greenland posture specifically undermines its own civilizational logic — Denmark meets every criterion the administration's conservatives claim to value — exposing aggressive nationalism as self-contradicting and capable of triggering rival nationalisms.

clusters.cluster_title: The redefinition of American identity from a creed-based to a blood-and-soil nation is historically wrong and politically dangerous — though defenders deny the 'blood and soil' characterization.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A sharp clash between the claim that America was founded on philosophy and liberal thought rather than ethnic belonging — making JD Vance's 'particular people' framing a dangerous departure — and the defense that the administration's view is more nuanced than the 'blood and soil' caricature and does not reduce to ethnic nationalism.

clusters.cluster_title: The shift from 'Western values' to 'Western civilization' as a framing device signals fundamentally different political projects — the Biden administration spoke of values, while Rubio invoked civilization 12 times.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The rhetorical shift from 'values' (universalist, creed-based) to 'civilization' (bounded, identity-based) marks a substantive change in the political project being pursued, not merely a linguistic variation — and tracking which term officials use reveals the underlying orientation of their foreign policy.

clusters.cluster_title: The West's current threat framing by the Trump administration invokes the NATO preamble's 'common heritage and civilization' but redirects the threat from external adversaries to internal progressive drift.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The NATO preamble's language about safeguarding 'liberty, common heritage and civilization' is being repurposed to frame the primary threat to the West not as Russian or Chinese aggression but as the post-Cold War progressive drift of Western institutions — a reorientation that is fiercely contested by those who see it as inverting the alliance's purpose.

themes.title: Disagreement within the West is not the crisis — the crisis is that the debate is increasingly conducted through coercion, imposition, and cancellation rather than open argument.
themes.abstract: Genuine civilizational differences between the US and Europe — especially on free speech traditions shaped by different historical traumas — are healthy and sustainable, but the attempt by either side to impose its norms on the other through vice-presidential speeches, AI-driven corporate standards, or rhetorical labeling of opponents as fascists transforms productive tension into destructive polarization and forecloses the vigorous open argument democracy requires.

clusters.cluster_title: There are genuine civilizational differences between the US and Europe — especially on free speech — and the current crisis is that one side is trying to impose its values on the other.
clusters.cluster_abstract: European hate-speech laws banning advocacy of fascism and communism reflect a different historical experience from the US's near-absolute free speech tradition, and the attempt by either side to impose its norms on the other — whether through vice-presidential speeches or AI-driven corporate speech standards — is unacceptable and threatens genuine sovereignty on both sides of the Atlantic.

clusters.cluster_title: The intra-Western debate is almost healthy — the real threat is when it is conducted through intimidation, bullying, and cancellation rather than vigorous open argument.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Disagreement within the West about values and institutions is not itself the problem; the danger arises when debate is suppressed through intimidation or rhetorical labeling — calling opponents fascists or public enemies — which divides society and prevents genuine engagement with opposing views.

themes.title: The Ukraine debate exposes an unresolved tension between moral accountability for aggression and pragmatic pursuit of peace, with each side accusing the other of foreclosing the path to settlement.
themes.abstract: Three positions — that Trump's negotiation push is a shameful betrayal, that arming Ukraine more aggressively would force better terms, and that peace without moral accountability for Russia's aggression destroys the normative foundations the West depends on — clash without resolution, each accusing the others of either prolonging suffering or surrendering principle, and the underlying question of whether durable peace can coexist with moral amnesty for the aggressor echoes the Versailles Treaty lesson that remains unlearned.

clusters.cluster_title: No viable alternative to negotiations exists for ending the Russia-Ukraine war on the battlefield — but there is an alternative: arming Ukraine to strike inside Russia and escalating the military cost to Putin.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A direct clash between the claim that critics of Trump's negotiation push offered no plan for ending the war militarily, and the counterproposal that providing Ukraine with Tomahawks, more Patriot missiles, and strike permission inside Russia would force Russia toward negotiations on better terms — with each side accusing the other of foreclosing the path to peace.

clusters.cluster_title: Trump's approach to Ukraine is a disgraceful betrayal of Western values, attempting to force a surrender to Putin — and it constitutes a betrayal of the NATO Charter and Universal Declaration of Human Rights.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The effort to push Ukraine into a deal that rewards Russian aggression is characterized as shameful and corrupt, driven by either ignorance or indifference to Ukrainian suffering, and it represents a fundamental break with the commitments encoded in the Atlantic Charter, the NATO preamble, and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

clusters.cluster_title: Lasting peace in Ukraine requires acknowledging moral responsibility for who started the war — peace without moral principle destroys the normative foundations that make the West meaningful.
clusters.cluster_abstract: You cannot achieve durable peace while destroying the moral integrity of the Ukrainian nation; any settlement that fails to assign responsibility for Russia's invasion undermines the normative framework the West depends on — echoing the Versailles Treaty lesson that historical clarity about aggression is non-negotiable for legitimacy.

themes.title: Disinformation and algorithmic media are top-tier threats to both domestic democracy and interstate diplomacy, and democracies are failing to use the regulatory tools they already possess.
themes.abstract: Social media's algorithmic reward of extreme positions destroys the shared factual basis and culture of compromise essential to democracy, while disinformation nearly derailed US-Colombia relations by overriding factual reality in high-stakes diplomacy — and governments' failure to enforce existing frameworks like the DMA and DSA makes this a democratic failure of will, not of capacity.

clusters.cluster_title: Disinformation is a top-tier global security risk that can destabilize bilateral relations, as demonstrated by the near-derailment of US-Colombia ties.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Misinformation and disinformation rank among the greatest global security risks and nearly destroyed US-Colombia relations when false narratives led President Trump to believe Colombia was doing nothing on counter-narcotics — a concrete case showing how information warfare can override factual reality in high-stakes diplomacy.

clusters.cluster_title: Social media's algorithmic destruction of shared facts and culture of compromise is the biggest structural threat to democratic discourse, yet democracies have failed to regulate it despite having the tools.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Social media rewards only extreme positions and eliminates the factual common ground and culture of compromise essential to democracy, and governments' failure to aggressively enforce existing regulatory frameworks like the DMA and DSA — partly due to caution about fast-moving technology — is itself a democratic failure.

themes.title: Multilateral institutions are simultaneously indispensable and paralyzed, and both unilateral US engagement and US withdrawal from multilateralism create dangerous vacuums.
themes.abstract: When affected populations exhaust all multilateral channels without result — as Venezuelan exiles did — US unilateral action fills a vacuum that multilateral institutions created through inaction; yet systematic US withdrawal from global compacts serves a strategy of authoritarian geographic consolidation. The paradox is that multilateral institutions are too weak to act but too important to abandon, and neither unilateralism nor re-engagement alone resolves the dilemma.

clusters.cluster_title: Venezuelan exiles welcomed US intervention because multilateral organizations had produced diagnosis but no concrete results, and the failure of conventional channels itself fuels populism.
clusters.cluster_abstract: When affected populations exhaust all conventional channels — elections, multilateral appeals — without result, US unilateral intervention fills a vacuum that multilateral institutions created through inaction, and this institutional failure is itself a driver of populism.

clusters.cluster_title: Trump's withdrawal from global compacts is designed to create an age of authoritarian geographic domains, and multilateral alliances are the hard stop against this fragmentation.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The systematic US withdrawal from global compacts and alliances serves a strategy of authoritarian consolidation in which Trump controls the Western Hemisphere and Putin bullies Europe, and re-engagement with multilateral institutions — especially trade agreements like TPP — is essential to preventing this outcome.

themes.title: Whether democratic legitimacy can scale beyond the nation-state is the unresolved structural question for European governance, with sovereigntists and federalists offering answers the other side rejects as category errors.
themes.abstract: The federalist prescription — a directly elected EU president and European army to spend projected defense budgets efficiently — and the sovereigntist insistence that only national governments provide the cultural proximity and accountability democracy requires represent not a policy disagreement but a philosophical one about where legitimacy resides, and neither side's institutional design can satisfy the other's foundational criterion.

clusters.cluster_title: The sovereign nation-state is contested as the sole guarantor of democracy: sovereigntists see supranational governance as a democratic deficit, while others argue the EU itself is democratically governed.
clusters.cluster_abstract: A fundamental disagreement over whether democratic legitimacy can exist beyond the nation-state — with one side arguing only national sovereignty provides the cultural continuity and proximity to voters that democracy requires, and the other insisting the EU's directly elected parliament and council of elected leaders constitute genuine democratic governance, not bureaucratic imposition.

clusters.cluster_title: Europe should pursue deeper integration — a directly elected EU president and a unified European army — to spend its projected €6.5 trillion in defense more efficiently and match American capabilities.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The long-term answer to both European security dependence on the US and the populist challenge is ambitious institutional deepening — a single elected EU president and a European army — framed as comparable in historical ambition to the euro or post-war Franco-German reconciliation.

themes.title: The norms governing how democracies engage with each other's internal politics have broken down from both directions — the US now supports populist candidates in allied countries while European institutions are accused of systematically excluding nationalist voices.
themes.abstract: Trump's reception of Poland's populist presidential candidate between election rounds and US funding of MAGA-aligned think tanks violate the longstanding norm against democratic interference in allied democracies, while the claimed vindication but continued exclusion of Orbán from EU decision-making represents a competing grievance about institutional gatekeeping — together revealing that the democratic community's internal rules of engagement have collapsed, with each side experiencing the other's actions as an illegitimate breach.

clusters.cluster_title: The old norm among allied democracies against interfering in each other's domestic politics has been broken by the Trump administration's support for populist candidates and MAGA-aligned think tanks in Europe.
clusters.cluster_abstract: Trump receiving Poland's populist presidential candidate between election rounds and US funding of MAGA-aligned think tanks in Europe violate the longstanding norm that democracies interfere only in autocracies on behalf of democracy — a reversal that will backfire by ending bipartisan pro-Americanism in historically allied countries.

clusters.cluster_title: Orbán was right 10 years ago on migration, family, and peace, and Europe's systematic exclusion of his voice from decision-making is a key conservative grievance.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The claim that Orbán anticipated the current consensus on migration and family policy and was vindicated by events — while being excluded from European Commission decision-making — serves as a central conservative grievance about institutional gatekeeping against nationalist voices in elite European forums.

themes.title: AI will displace more people and identities than migration ever could, rendering the West's current identity debates incomplete and forcing a redefinition of nation and civilization itself.
themes.abstract: The existential anxiety driving populist politics runs deeper than immigration or economic inequality: AI will replace jobs and identities on a scale that makes current debates look parochial, and no existing framework — national, civilizational, or values-based — has begun to grapple with what political community means when the fundamental relationship between people and productive labor is severed.

clusters.cluster_title: AI is 'the other great replacement' — it will displace more people than migrants ever could and force a redefinition of nation, civilization, and the West itself.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The existential anxiety behind populist politics runs deeper than immigration alone: AI will replace jobs and identities on a scale that makes current migration debates look parochial, forcing a wholesale rethinking of what a nation and a civilization mean — rendering the West's current identity conflicts incomplete.

themes.title: Allowing nuclear arms control to lapse and contemplating resumed testing represent dangerous reversals that demand urgent reinstatement regardless of the difficulty of extending the framework to China.
themes.abstract: The expiration of the New START Treaty — after the Trump administration refused a Russian offer to extend it — and the suggestion of resuming underground nuclear testing reverse decades of arms control progress, and the aspiration to bring China into a new framework does not justify abandoning the existing bilateral restraints that prevented worst-case escalation.

clusters.cluster_title: Allowing the New START Treaty to expire and contemplating resumed nuclear testing are major strategic mistakes that demand urgent reinstatement of nuclear arms control.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The expiration of the New START Treaty — after the Trump administration refused a Russian offer to extend it — and the suggestion of resuming underground nuclear testing represent dangerous reversals of decades of arms control progress that must be corrected, even if extending the framework to China remains aspirational.

themes.title: The panel's own dynamics reproduced the power asymmetry it was analyzing, demonstrating that even self-aware elite forums replicate the exclusion patterns they critique.
themes.abstract: The moderator's acknowledgment that the session on the international order gave inadequate floor time to Saudi and Colombian voices enacted the very structural imbalance the discussion was meant to interrogate — revealing that the concentration of discursive power among dominant Western actors persists even when the explicit agenda is to challenge it.

clusters.cluster_title: The panel's own dynamics reproduced the power asymmetry it was analyzing, with the two largest blocs dominating while middle powers were marginalized.
clusters.cluster_abstract: The moderator acknowledged that the session on the international order was wholly dominated by US and EU voices, failing to give adequate floor time to the Saudi and Colombian representatives — enacting the very structural imbalance the discussion was supposed to interrogate.
